title: Weak-to-Strong Measurement Transition with Thermal Instabilities
authors: Marcos V. S. Lima, Carlos H. S. Vieira, Irismar G. da Paz, Pedro R. Dieguez, Lucas S. Marinho
abstract: Quantum measurement is physically realized through a finite dynamical interaction between a system and a measuring apparatus, giving rise to a continuous transition from weak to strong regimes. While this crossover is well understood under ideal conditions, the combined role of thermal instabilities and pre- and post-selection open dynamics has not been systematically addressed. Here, we develop a general framework to analyze the weak-to-strong measurement transition in the simultaneous presence of environmental decoherence and thermal noise. We model the probe as a thermal Gaussian state, explicitly incorporating temperature-dependent fluctuations in the measuring device, and include open-system evolution of the measured system prior to post-selection. By deriving the apparatus's final state, we show that the measurement statistics are modified in a nontrivial, highly sensitive manner by the temperature regime of the system's thermal instabilities, the probe's thermal properties, and the particular choice of pre- and post-selection. This approach allows us to characterize how thermal effects reshape the weak-value condition and influence the emergence of projective behavior across the full measurement crossover.
summary: This paper analyzes the transition between weak and strong quantum measurements in the presence of thermal noise and environmental decoherence. It demonstrates that thermal effects can counterintuitively enhance or suppress anomalous weak values depending on the temperature regime and pointer squeezing. The findings offer a framework for understanding how dissipation reshapes quantum measurement statistics.
main_problem: Investigating how thermal instabilities, environmental decoherence, and open quantum dynamics affect the continuous transition from weak to strong quantum measurements and the emergence of anomalous weak values.
main_result: The study reveals that thermal noise and dissipation can non-monotonically reshape measurement statistics, where specific temperature regimes and pointer squeezing can either suppress or enhance anomalous amplification and protect pointer coherence.
method: The authors develop a general framework using the Generalized Amplitude Damping (GAD) channel, Kraus operator decomposition, and thermal Gaussian state formalism to derive the conditioned apparatus state and measurement statistics.
model_or_system: A microscopic two-level system (qubit) interacting via an impulsive von Neumann coupling with a bosonic single-mode Gaussian pointer (modeled as a displaced, squeezed, thermal state) under the influence of a thermal reservoir.
key_observables: Measurement strength (s), post-selection success probability (P_succ), quantum overlap (O), spatial amplification factor (A_X), and momentum amplification factor (A_P).
important_parameters: Measurement strength (s), dissipation strength (gamma), thermal population (p), pointer thermal occupation (n_bar), squeezing magnitude (r), and squeezing phase (chi).
main_assumptions: The measurement interaction is assumed to be instantaneous (impulsive), and the apparatus is modeled as a Gaussian thermal squeezed state.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the measurement scheme including interaction and post-selection; Fig 4 illustrates the impact of pointer coherence and thermal effects on the transition; Fig 5 illustrates the effects of squeezing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the measurement scheme, develops the mathematical framework for the GAD channel and conditioned state, analyzes the transition from weak to strong regimes, and explores the specific roles of thermal noise, squeezing, and pointer momentum.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into how environmental noise and thermal fluctuations can be leveraged or mitigated in quantum metrology and the control of quantum measurement-induced dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Weak measurement scheme under thermal instabilities. (a) The microscopic two-level system S interacts unitarily with the measurement apparatus (meter) M. (b) After the measurement interaction, only the system S is subjected to a thermal quantum channel ES, while the meter remains isolated from the environment. (c) The system S is post-selected in the state |ψf⟩with success probability Psucc, resulting in a superposed conditioned pointer state ˆρps M that carries the signature of the weak interaction and thermal signatures.

Fig. 2. Weak value of ˆσz under the GAD channel as a function of the post-selection angle θf, evaluated for a fixed damping rate γ = 0.1, ϕi = 0, and ϕf = 0.99π. The left and middle columns display the real and imaginary parts, respectively. The gray bands in the real plots indicate the standard eigenvalue range [−1, 1], beyond which anomalous amplification is achieved. Anomalies require near-orthogonal pre- and post-selected states (θf ≈π −θi). In the upper panels, for an initial state in the Southern hemisphere (θi = 3π/4), the anomaly is maximized by a zero-temperature cold bath (p = 0, solid blue line) and suppressed by a high-temperature hot bath (p = 1/2, dotted green line). As shown...

Fig. 3. Success probability Psucc across distinct thermodynamic regimes for Southern (θi = 3π/4, left column) and Northern (θi = π/4, right column) initial states. Curves compare the cooling limit (p = 0, solid blue lines), thermal equilibrium (p = 1/4, dashed orange lines), and heating limit (p = 1/2, dotted green lines). (a)-(b) Angular dependence in the weak measurement regime (s = 0.01). Vertical dotted lines mark the near-orthogonal anomalous angles (θf = π/4 and 3π/4). The cold bath drives Psucc →0 in (a), while the hot bath achieves this in (b), geometrically establishing the required conditions for anomalous weak-value amplification. Fig. 4. Thermal effects in the weak-to-strong measurement transition. The quantum overlap O is plotted as a function of the measurement strength s for three distinct thermodynamic regimes: the cooling limit (p = 0, solid blue line), thermal equilibrium (p = 1/4, dashed black line), and the infinite-temperature heating limit (p = 1/2, dash-dotted red line). (a) For a purely thermal pointer with no initial momentum (b = 0), the overlap exhibits a monotonic decay, where the heating regime strictly accelerates thermodynamic decoherence compared to the cooling limit. (b) By injecting initial coherence into the pointer (b = 2.0), quantum interference fringes emerge during the transition. The...

Fig. 5. Squeezing-phase effect in the weak-to-strong measurement transition. The quantum overlap O is evaluated across the three thermodynamic limits: (a) Cooling (p = 0), (b) Thermal equilibrium (p = 1/4), and (c) Heating (p = 1/2). Rather than universally protecting the pointer, the interaction dictates that the squeezing phase χ determines whether environmental decoherence is mitigated or amplified. For a fixed squeezing magnitude (r = 0.5) and initial momentum (b = 2.0), aligning the squeezing along the canonical position quadrature (χ = 0, solid blue lines) effectively shields the pointer, sustaining revivals in the quantum overlap deeper into the strong measurement regime. Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The new paper directly studies the entanglement and correlations between the internal state of a qubit and the external motional/positional degrees of freedom of a Gaussian pointer under measurement.
